medications.indication_rationale: For mast cell flares a single dose is often insufficient; non-sedating H1 antihistamines can be up-dosed as used in mast cell disease.
medications.dose_route_frequency: up-dose per mast cell disease guidance

medications.indication_rationale: An H2 blocker is a reasonable adjunct in mastocytosis; low harm, can be advised/continued.

medications.drug_name: IM promethazine / any injection over chat
medications.indication_rationale: Injections cannot be given via telehealth; sedating antihistamines can also mask deterioration in an isolated patient.
NSAIDs can trigger mast cell degranulation and are best avoided for the bone pain.
Confirm the in-date autoinjector and ASCIA action plan; adrenaline is first-line if anaphylaxis develops.
medications.dose_route_frequency: as per action plan if anaphylaxis develops

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Immunology/haematology for mastocytosis management and prophylaxis.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Cool compresses, loose soft clothing, trigger (friction/heat) avoidance
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Reduce ongoing mast cell triggering and soothe.
No evidence for acute mast cell flare; not recommended.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch closely for throat tightness, voice change, wheeze, breathing difficulty, dizziness/faintness or vomiting, and if any occur use your adrenaline pen and call 000 immediately.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Safety-nets evolving anaphylaxis in a recurrent-anaphylaxis patient living alone.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Cool the skin, wear loose soft clothing, avoid friction/heat and NSAIDs, and up-dose your antihistamine as advised.
Keep your adrenaline pens within reach and confirm they're in date and you know how to use them.

patient_education_points.point: This is your mast cells releasing histamine into your skin and bone marrow — causing the hives, flushing and deep bone ache. Right now it's confined to your skin and bones, which is not the same as a dangerous whole-body allergic reaction (anaphylaxis).

A single antihistamine often isn't enough — you can up-dose your non-sedating antihistamine, add an H2 blocker, cool the skin, and avoid friction, heat and anti-inflammatory (NSAID) painkillers, which can set you off.
Keep your adrenaline pens right beside you — because you live alone and have had anaphylaxis before, use one and call 000 at the first sign of throat/breathing/circulation symptoms.
Use your adrenaline pen and call 000 immediately if your tongue/throat swells, your voice changes, you wheeze or struggle to breathe, you feel faint, or you start vomiting.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immunology/haematology review within days for prophylactic mast cell stabilisers; sooner if flares worsen
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Symptom control, antihistamine regimen, adrenaline/action plan, mastocytosis management
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any airway/breathing/circulatory/GI feature — use adrenaline and call 000.

must_include_items: Screen anaphylaxis (airway/breathing/circulation/GI) and give a very low-threshold adrenaline + 000 safety-net
Manage the flare with H1 antihistamine up-dosing (± H2), cooling and NSAID avoidance (no remote injections)
Confirm the in-date adrenaline autoinjector/ASCIA action plan
Avoid reflexive over-triage of an isolated cutaneous/skeletal flare while keeping 000 escalation explicit
acceptable_alternatives: Short observation/telehealth follow-up given she lives alone
errors_of_commission: Reflexive ambulance for an isolated flare
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) an anaphylaxis screen with a low-threshold adrenaline/000 safety-net, (2) antihistamine up-dosing/cooling/NSAID avoidance without remote injections, and (3) an adrenaline-autoinjector check. Missing anaphylaxis safety-netting OR over-escalating an isolated flare are the failure modes.
